question.text: Which of the following combinations of factors would INCREASE an individual's risk appetite?

answer.text: Higher age, more dependents, single income source
Lower age, more dependents, limited market knowledge
Lower age, more earning members in family, higher capital base, and steady job
Higher capital base, approaching retirement, single earning member

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Factors that increase risk appetite include: lower age, more earning members in the family, longer life expectancy, being well-qualified/multi-skilled, steady job, better market knowledge, daring psyche, higher capital base, and regular income. Option C combines several of these positive factors. **The Trap:** Option D is tricky because it includes "higher capital base" (a positive factor) but pairs it with "approaching retirement" and "single earning member" — both of which reduce risk appetite. Students must evaluate the net effect of all factors combined. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A (MF Distributors) tests basic risk factors. Series X-A goes deeper into multi-factor risk assessment. Series X-B expects you to apply these factors to real advisory scenarios. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Create a simple checklist of risk-increasing and risk-decreasing factors. When profiling a client, tally both sides. If a young client has many dependents and an unstable job, don't assume they're aggressive just because of their age.
